title: Wetland water-treatment study notes — sustainability triple-bottom-line
html:
Wetland water-treatment study notes

Source: 2022-04-21 Apple Note. OSU coursework notes — looks like exam prep or a class-discussion outline for an environmental-engineering or sustainability class focused on constructed-wetland water treatment (likely the Jackson Frazier wetland near Corvallis, which Yahir flags by name in his Oct 2022 todo dump).

TL;DR

  • Why wetlands: nutrient removal, oxygen addition (waterfalls aerate), and temperature control (people heat water by showering, by being people; wetlands cool it back down). Aesthetic is the primary stated reason; cooling + oxygen are secondary benefits.
  • Three plant criteria: must be native, must tolerate constant wet, must contribute to "cooking the water" (Yahir's notes; presumably means biological filtration).
  • Resilient plant community = adaptive: survives seasonal change, environmental pressure, disease. Yahir captured the resilience definition cleanly — a frame he'll reuse in product/people contexts later.
  • Triple-bottom-line scorecard (environmental / social / economic, the standard sustainability framework):
    • Environmental — pros: salmon population stability, wildlife habitat, carbon cycle, flood control. Cons: leaks, invasive species, mosquitoes.
    • Social — pros: pleasant scenery. Cons: allergies, overcrowding, animal conflicts.
    • Economic — pros: property values up (Yahir notes the gentrification flip himself: "could be positive for some but negative to those who are lower social class and can't afford to live anymore"), Albany avoids fines. Cons: expensive build, no revenue.

Pattern observed

Yahir is already doing systems-thinking pros/cons-with-tradeoffs at age 19/20 — and the gentrification-flip aside (property-value pro that becomes a class-displacement con) is the sort of adjacent-effect thinking that shows up in his much later Austin and AI search takes. Same instinct, applied to wetlands.

This is a small node but worth keeping connected — it's the earliest coursework artifact in the vault that shows the systems-thinking habit forming inside a class assignment.

Key snippets

  • "Have some process of cooking the water." (Yahir's gloss for biological filtration)
  • "It can survive all the seasons, it can adapt to pressure."
  • "Could be positive for some but negative to those who are lower social class."
